case_name: Sharee Howard v. Town of Swanzey
transcript_text:
This is case 2025 is 0155 Sherry Howard versus town of Swansea.

Good morning. For the record, Justice Will is unable to be with us physically present today.

He will be reviewing the recording of the argument and will participate in our deliberations.

Attorney Dowd, I understand you reserved two minutes.

Yes, I have.

Please proceed.

Good morning. May it please the court. I'm Attorney Kelly Dowd.

I'm representing Sherry Howard in this case.

This case presents a question to the court.

In this case, there's a proposed use of land.

And there is a legal question of whether this use of the land either falls under the category of industrial park,

or is this a description of professional and business offices.

So that's the legal question. And then there's a secondary question of what are the consequences of which box,

when we're looking at what boxes should fall under, what are the consequences?

Well, the consequences in the zoning is if it's an industrial park,

this is a use that's permitted by special exception in the district.

So it's not that this is a not permitted use. It's not that to do an industrial park.

You require variance that you have to make a showing of hardship.

It's simply permitted by special exception.

If it's a business office development type thing, then it is a permitted use of the zone.

I think important to this, and there's some discussion.

I'm not sure there's some statements in the opposing brief that the

Palin here is speculating.

If we go to the certified record, page 19, there's an application from all-purpose storage.

And they describe the use as services and it states to be rented out to various service businesses

and parenthetically states plumbing, HVAC, landscaping and electrical.

So what they claim that they are proposing is to rent the space out to these service businesses.

Which are businesses?

Which are businesses?

Commercial businesses.

What's that?

Commercial businesses.

They're commercial businesses, yes.

And then the second piece is on page 30 of the certified record,

there's a gentleman, Brandon speaking for all-purpose storage to the board,

states that, explaining the use, says, this is light industrial.

There may be some small fabrication and it's more likely to host commercial types

like plumbing, electricians and roofing companies.

That's on page 30.

So we have these service businesses and also there's fabrication.

There's going to be some light industrial fabrication on the location.

Do you agree that we should not be addressing whether this is a service?

Yes.

Yeah.

There was an appeal.

The initial proposal was that this was a service business.

It was an appeal to the ZBA.

The ZBA determined it wasn't services.

They determined it was under this business offices category.

But that decision that it's not services was never appealed and never perfected

and that was correctly addressed by the trial court and the trial court's order.

What I would say is this court and what this issue depends upon

is the Pele, the town in all-purpose storage,

wants to conflate the concept of a place of business with the concept of an office.

So, for example, if you go to a restaurant,

Applebee's, Longhorn Steakhouse or whatever, there's a place of business.

You walk into the place of business.

Now, at that restaurant, there may be a manager's office in the back somewhere,

but you're not going to the office, you're going to this place of business.

In the operations of the business, Applebee's is not an office, a business office.

It is a restaurant.

And if you had a district that permitted offices and didn't permit restaurants to not be permitted.

In terms of, if we look at the proposed use,

and the ZBA in this case said, oh, this is offices,

the one word you won't see in any of what they say in their application is that any of this is offices.

And that's true, particularly in professional services.

If you have professional services, you have an accountant or a lawyer or a doctor,

the place of business is called an office.

But nobody, I would challenge the court to tell us

when you've seen the offices of John Smith Electrician

or the offices of so-and-so, that's not the way this works.

And so...

Well, let's stop there.

I used to drive by the offices of Bo Plumbing and Heating every day.

Yes.

And they were offices. They did their billing there.

They dispatched from there.

Yes.

Why isn't that an office, even though it's associated with a not,

arguably a non-professional type of business?

No, I agree with the court that if you have a large manufacturing plant,

you're going to have a floor where manufacturing takes place

and you're going to have...

Any kind of business is going to have some office space.

But what is being done in this case is,

and if you look at page 17 of the opposing brief,

there's this discussion about workshops versus offices.

But I'm not sure that given that the all-purpose storage,

it says they're going to be conducting light industrial manufacturing.

I don't know how that somehow is not a workshop

if you're producing manufacturing goods in a particular location.

Would that be an example though of an entity that might need a further approval?

No, it's either an industrial park or its offices.

If that's not offices, that needs to be a condition of the approval, right?

Or, you know, I mean, it's not a subsequent thing.

I guess the way I read it is that if it's a business

following the trial court's reasoning,

then it can exist in that location.

But there was a caveat that there might need to be further approval

depending on the exact use.

So a plumber might be able to function under this approval.

But somebody engaging, I'm speculating right now,

in light manufacturing might need a further approval from the town.

Do you disagree?

There's two parts of the way that land use is regulated.

You have zoning and you have site plan.

I'm very well aware of that.

So what I'm saying is you may have site plan requirements,

but if you're saying, no, these are business offices,

they're doing light industrial manufacturing and that's a business office,

then you can't on the base of the zoning power say, oh, you can't do that.

And if the zoning board intended to put some kind of restriction on it,

that should have been a condition in that approval if they wanted to do that.

Now, the other thing that's interesting about the trial court's order though

is zoning, there's a definition of what they call corporate office.

Doesn't say business office and there's nothing in the table of uses

that talks about a corporate office, but I think I made the argument brief

and I think it's clear that not in law,

which we understand corporations are a type of business structure,

but usually in common parlance people talk about corporate and business identically.

But what they say corporate offices is that buildings or building

used primarily for conducting the affairs of a business,

profession, service, industry, government or like activities.

So this conducting the affairs of language.

And as you pointed out, there was a, you know, you can have a plumbing business

and you can have an office where they conduct the affairs of the plumbing business.

Any kind of business is going to have some office function,

but an office is this place where conducting the affairs takes place.

And I pointed out that in the case law of this court,

when this court has referred to conducting the affairs,

you're talking about high level managerial decisions and administrative decisions.

So it follows based on our case law here and the cases are cited in the brief

that this office where the affairs are conducted

is going to be this high level managerial decision making process.

It's not going to be a large bay with a bunch of heavy equipment

and trucks and materials and then in the corner somewhere,

you're going to have a computer to do billing or something.

Well, there's a business office, the term business office,

broader than the term corporate office,

which denotes a corporate suite, you know,

where you have the various officers of the corporation.

Whereas with a small business, there is no suite,

but there's still office functions.

This has this definition of corporate office.

And the thing is a corporate office,

a corporation is a form of business, right?

It's a form of ownership of property

as compared to like a limited partnership or something.

There's no reference to the structure of the business in this definition.

So this would seem to be the best definition for constructing

what do they mean by professional or business office.

It's a place where you're conducting the affairs.

Now, place of location,

just where the place of business is not synonymous with an office.

Although, yes, if you have a place of business,

I do want to kind of tie in with one thing.

There is a statement made that in these businesses,

there's going to be essentially a mobile workshop

that they're going out in the field somewhere.

And that, you know, to the extent that they're doing warehousing

of materials and equipment in these bays,

that that's accessory.

But the issue is this is land use regulations.

We're looking at regulating a piece of land.

Nobody cares what someone's doing in Keen.

What's happening on the land is they're essentially,

primarily warehousing equipment and materials and tools

that they're going to then take out somewhere else

and do this workshop.

Well, wouldn't you agree with me that if this is approved

as use as a business office,

and one of the tenants is using the property in a way

that's inconsistent with that,

that the town would have enforcement ability

to remedy that situation.

You can't get approval for one use

and then engage in a different use that is more intensive

than what was originally represented to the ZBA, right?

No, because this case is about whether this idea

of having it limited to business offices has any meaning at all

or if it's just synonymous with any location where a business is.

And so if this court is going to agree with the town

and the interveners that business office means anything

if you have a business, you could have, you know,

manufacturing thing, and as long as you have a little office

and back, you can do anything there,

then it's essentially meaning.

I mean, this is the problem with words have no meaning,

then they mean everything or they mean nothing.

So, no, you can't enforce something that has no real meaning.

Well, even if we agree with you,

the Pellee here could still apply for a special exception.

That's correct.

And is that the path that you think they should have taken?

Yes.

Yeah, that is the path.

That's the path.

And I mean, they say in our brief that we're saying,

oh, they can't do this use there.

We disagree completely.

It's a permitted use, but it's permitted by special exception.

And I think because these uses tend to be smelly,

there's grease, there's heavy equipment, there's noise,

there's things, we think that that probably reflects

why the zoning board or, you know, when they adopted

these ordinance that maybe we want a little more scrutiny

or something abutting a residential neighborhood,

like this does, maybe we want a little more scrutiny

before we put in something that's going to potentially

cause a lot of noise, there may be smoke,

there may be exhaust, there may be other things,

there may be hazardous materials and chemicals and whatnot

versus, you know, if you have a dentist or something there

or a law firm or an accountant,

you don't have the same kinds of land use regulatory concerns.

You think that is that consistent with our standard of review?

Aren't you asking us to replace our judgment

for that of the ZBA and the trial court?

No, this is an interpretation of the zoning ordinance

and you guys have stated that that's a question of law

and this court refused that de novo

and I think it's pretty clear de novo looking at

what the definition of corporate office is

in constructing professional business offices

that this is clearly an industrial park,

not an office and just because you have a place

of law, I'm kidding this,

but just because you have a place of business

it doesn't make equal that's office.

Thank you attorney, attorney Driscoll.

Good morning your honours ma'am, please the court.

My name is Rich Driscoll

and I represent the Intervenor All Purpose Storage.

The question before the court is whether a trade business

can establish a business office

in Sweeney's Business District.

The answer is yes.

The court should begin with the text of the ordinance.

Section 5B1E provides

that professional and business offices

are permitted in that district.

In the petitioner's brief she focuses on

the word professional and office

but ignores the words and business.

A plumbing and an electrical company

is plainly a business

and nothing in the ordinance restricts

the use of the land to white collar professions.

It's a business but is it an office?

I mean that's the issue that

attorney Doud is talking about.

How is this an office?

Well, I think the petitioner's argument

is really focusing on

offices are typically where paperwork is done

and the emphasis in the brief

is that that is traditionally

like a white collar space.

And we respond that

that's not necessarily the case.

Blue collar businesses

like plumbers and electricians

also require places

to do their paperwork and invoicing.

The nature of the business

has nothing to do with how the space

is being used.

And so while the proposed space

is going to be rented to

trade businesses

there's nothing in the record

to support the conclusion

that these spaces are going to be

other than business offices.

Now I understand that there are

some points in the certified record

where there were references

to potentially

some light manufacturing

or I believe it was light assembly

was the language there.

But that doesn't transform this space

from an office into

an industrial use.

Should we look for a definition of office?

Should we look to the definition

of corporate office?

I think the court

to justice schools point

a business office is more broad than a corporate office

but the definition

in the ordinance of corporate office

isn't too far off

from the general definition

of an office. It's a place

where business conducts its affairs.

And so

you know the court should just

in its ordinary courts apply the plain language

but it can consider

the definition of corporate office

to inform its decision.

Speaking of the

ordinance overall

what's the difference between

a professional and business office

and

a professional business office

as used in the village business district?

So a professional

business office

is an office for

lawyers, accountants, doctors

things that are traditionally thought of as professional

which the ordinance

itself defines the term professional

to include those kinds of work.

Why would you use the word business?

Why isn't it just a professional office?

I think in the context

of the ordinance

that you're looking at, I think that section 5a1d

the word professional

modifies business

and so it's

a professional type business

office.

It's different from the section that we're asking

the court to interpret in this case

because there it says a professional

and business office

and so the conjunctive and

separates the idea of professional

from business. Those are two separate ideas

and those words each

independently modify the word office.

So here the question is whether

the proposed use is

a professional office or it could be

a business office and here we're saying that

these are business offices.

The purpose of the business

district is to provide

commercial retail office and similar

uses to serving the needs of the community

and allowing businesses such as plumbers

HVAC

electricians

is consistent with the

stated purpose of the

of the business district

and so restricting the use of the

office space based on the nature

of the work that's being conducted

is improper.

It seems like the types of

businesses that

were listed in connection with the application

are the sorts of businesses

where almost all of the

physical work is done away from

the headquarters of the office

or wherever the

trucks are stored and so forth.

By their nature they're going off-site

to do the work.

If one of these

units was rented to a machine shop

where in fact

machining was being done

even if there's an office in the back

do you have a view

as to whether that would require

additional approval from the town?

I think under those

circumstances a machine shop

could arguably be considered

closer to an industrial

setting than

HVAC plumbing electrical work.

Those are

and this is not an issue before the court

but those are as you were describing service businesses

where the work is performed generally

off-site and the purpose of the

site is to

maybe store some equipment

house paperwork

it's a place where billing can be completed

where invoicing is done

where customers can call and

dispatch

service technicians

so these units

are they climate control?

I don't know the answer to that

I do know that there are

bathroom units in each one of these

units.

Hopefully they're climate control.

Another challenge

to the petitioner's case

is that the tenants are

currently unknown at this time

there isn't

anyone in the record who is

known to use

any one of the individual units

and without knowing the specific

use that's proposed by each tenant

this court

cannot conclude

that the purpose

or that the actual use

is in violation of this ordinance

because

if for example we have the example

of a machine shop coming in

that would give us different facts

that would allow the court to analyze

whether that actual use

is violating the ordinance.

Are there any conditions attached to this approval?

Well

the tenants

would need to either comply with

the business office

requirement under the ordinance

or as the trial court

and the ZBA suggested

and this is on the trial court's

order at its native page 8

if a proposed use

was going to be

different or expanded

beyond just this business office

there's an opportunity for the tenant to seek

a variance from the town

and at that point the town would have

the opportunity to evaluate

whether that

specific proposed use is consistent

with the overall character

of the land and the neighborhood

and doesn't the town have enforcement

authority where the use is different

from as represented

in the application

in the area before the ZBA?

Absolutely.

So if an actual use

is contrary to the proposed use

the town would have an enforcement

capacity to enforce

the ordinance under this application.

And the issue of conditions was never presented

to the board?

I don't believe so your Honor.

Because the ordinance is

plain

that this

land allows

professional and business offices

and because the proposed uses

HVAC

electricians plumbers

and similar businesses

are businesses within that definition

the court should conclude

that those are business offices that are permitted

in this district

and furthermore because

there is no evidence in the record

to support

the conclusion

that any actual use is going to

violate this ordinance

there is no basis

to reverse

the trial course decision

and briefly before I conclude

Justice Donovan you were asking whether

the

sort of the list of things that

the petitioner was identifying

that there's chemicals and smells and other things

and that this is more of an

industrial characteristic

I think by presenting those arguments

that is asking the court to change

its standard of review.

Here is, of course, Donovan with respect

to the meaning of the ordinance

but with respect to the factual findings

that the ZBA and the trial court made

those must be deferential

and none of those factors were considered

by the trial court or the ZBA

which could have supported

the decision to deny the project.

So for these reasons

we ask that this court affirm the trial

course decision.

Turn you down.

I just want to say one thing

about enforcement. You heard from

the intervener's attorney

that pretty much

doing light manufacturing

there may not

accord with the ordinance.

However, you have

an application to this land use board

saying this is our proposed use. You have

the representative of all purpose

on the record saying we're going to do

light manufacturing here

and the court

essentially I think

suggesting that oh well can't

if they start doing light manufacturing

can't the town do some enforcement

and the answer is no.

If someone comes out and says hey I'm going to do

some light manufacturing here

and then you approve them

and then they start doing light manufacturing

and then you try to enforce it

you're going to get hit within a stop

claim. They relied on

this board. They told the board what they're

going to do if the board turns around

and tries to enforce. At that point

you don't have difficulties with that

so that's not an issue here

and that's the concern here

in addition there's this claim that

we don't know what the actual use

is going to be. Well

when you get a variance you get any land use

approval. You don't know what the final building

is going to look like or what's going to happen

you go on what an applicant

represents to the board

and I think what the court needs to rely on

is what the applicant represented to the board

both in the application which is

page 19 of the certified record

and the testimony of page 30 that they're

going to be doing light industrial use

and I think when you consider all those pieces

in this this is clearly

an industrial part. We're not

talking about professional offices, this isn't

dentist, it's not a bookkeeper

it's not somebody doing

massage therapy or Reiki

or something. It is

these are industrial

they're warehousing equipment and tools

they're going out to other places and performing

work or doing manufacturing

so I don't know

Is light manufacturing defined

under the zoning ordinance?

Is light manufacturing defined

under the zoning ordinance? There is a definition

of industrial part

and I believe that includes

but I off the top of my head I do not

believe that the difference between light

I think the point is these are I think

30 by 60

bays and I guess you could run multiple

bays but you know you're

going to be hard pressed to be

running an oil refinery

in a 30 by 60 bay

guess what

Thank you.

In case submitted.

segments.text: This is case 2025 is 0155 Sherry Howard versus town of Swansea.
Good morning. For the record, Justice Will is unable to be with us physically present today.
He will be reviewing the recording of the argument and will participate in our deliberations.
Attorney Dowd, I understand you reserved two minutes.
Yes, I have.
Please proceed.
Good morning. May it please the court. I'm Attorney Kelly Dowd.
I'm representing Sherry Howard in this case.
This case presents a question to the court.
In this case, there's a proposed use of land.
And there is a legal question of whether this use of the land either falls under the category of industrial park,
or is this a description of professional and business offices.
So that's the legal question. And then there's a secondary question of what are the consequences of which box,
when we're looking at what boxes should fall under, what are the consequences?
Well, the consequences in the zoning is if it's an industrial park,
this is a use that's permitted by special exception in the district.
So it's not that this is a not permitted use. It's not that to do an industrial park.
You require variance that you have to make a showing of hardship.
It's simply permitted by special exception.
If it's a business office development type thing, then it is a permitted use of the zone.
I think important to this, and there's some discussion.
I'm not sure there's some statements in the opposing brief that the
Palin here is speculating.
If we go to the certified record, page 19, there's an application from all-purpose storage.
And they describe the use as services and it states to be rented out to various service businesses
and parenthetically states plumbing, HVAC, landscaping and electrical.
So what they claim that they are proposing is to rent the space out to these service businesses.
Which are businesses?
Which are businesses?
Commercial businesses.
What's that?
Commercial businesses.
They're commercial businesses, yes.
And then the second piece is on page 30 of the certified record,
there's a gentleman, Brandon speaking for all-purpose storage to the board,
states that, explaining the use, says, this is light industrial.
There may be some small fabrication and it's more likely to host commercial types
like plumbing, electricians and roofing companies.
That's on page 30.
So we have these service businesses and also there's fabrication.
There's going to be some light industrial fabrication on the location.
Do you agree that we should not be addressing whether this is a service?
Yes.
Yeah.
There was an appeal.
The initial proposal was that this was a service business.
It was an appeal to the ZBA.
The ZBA determined it wasn't services.
They determined it was under this business offices category.
But that decision that it's not services was never appealed and never perfected
and that was correctly addressed by the trial court and the trial court's order.
What I would say is this court and what this issue depends upon
is the Pele, the town in all-purpose storage,
wants to conflate the concept of a place of business with the concept of an office.
So, for example, if you go to a restaurant,
Applebee's, Longhorn Steakhouse or whatever, there's a place of business.
You walk into the place of business.
Now, at that restaurant, there may be a manager's office in the back somewhere,
but you're not going to the office, you're going to this place of business.
In the operations of the business, Applebee's is not an office, a business office.
It is a restaurant.
And if you had a district that permitted offices and didn't permit restaurants to not be permitted.
In terms of, if we look at the proposed use,
and the ZBA in this case said, oh, this is offices,
the one word you won't see in any of what they say in their application is that any of this is offices.
And that's true, particularly in professional services.
If you have professional services, you have an accountant or a lawyer or a doctor,
the place of business is called an office.
But nobody, I would challenge the court to tell us
when you've seen the offices of John Smith Electrician
or the offices of so-and-so, that's not the way this works.
And so...
Well, let's stop there.
I used to drive by the offices of Bo Plumbing and Heating every day.
Yes.
And they were offices. They did their billing there.
They dispatched from there.
Yes.
Why isn't that an office, even though it's associated with a not,
arguably a non-professional type of business?
No, I agree with the court that if you have a large manufacturing plant,
you're going to have a floor where manufacturing takes place
and you're going to have...
Any kind of business is going to have some office space.
But what is being done in this case is,
and if you look at page 17 of the opposing brief,
there's this discussion about workshops versus offices.
But I'm not sure that given that the all-purpose storage,
it says they're going to be conducting light industrial manufacturing.
I don't know how that somehow is not a workshop
if you're producing manufacturing goods in a particular location.
Would that be an example though of an entity that might need a further approval?
No, it's either an industrial park or its offices.
If that's not offices, that needs to be a condition of the approval, right?
Or, you know, I mean, it's not a subsequent thing.
I guess the way I read it is that if it's a business
following the trial court's reasoning,
then it can exist in that location.
But there was a caveat that there might need to be further approval
depending on the exact use.
So a plumber might be able to function under this approval.
But somebody engaging, I'm speculating right now,
in light manufacturing might need a further approval from the town.
Do you disagree?
There's two parts of the way that land use is regulated.
You have zoning and you have site plan.
I'm very well aware of that.
So what I'm saying is you may have site plan requirements,
but if you're saying, no, these are business offices,
they're doing light industrial manufacturing and that's a business office,
then you can't on the base of the zoning power say, oh, you can't do that.
And if the zoning board intended to put some kind of restriction on it,
that should have been a condition in that approval if they wanted to do that.
Now, the other thing that's interesting about the trial court's order though
is zoning, there's a definition of what they call corporate office.
Doesn't say business office and there's nothing in the table of uses
that talks about a corporate office, but I think I made the argument brief
and I think it's clear that not in law,
which we understand corporations are a type of business structure,
but usually in common parlance people talk about corporate and business identically.
But what they say corporate offices is that buildings or building
used primarily for conducting the affairs of a business,
profession, service, industry, government or like activities.
So this conducting the affairs of language.
And as you pointed out, there was a, you know, you can have a plumbing business
and you can have an office where they conduct the affairs of the plumbing business.
Any kind of business is going to have some office function,
but an office is this place where conducting the affairs takes place.
And I pointed out that in the case law of this court,
when this court has referred to conducting the affairs,
you're talking about high level managerial decisions and administrative decisions.
So it follows based on our case law here and the cases are cited in the brief
that this office where the affairs are conducted
is going to be this high level managerial decision making process.
It's not going to be a large bay with a bunch of heavy equipment
and trucks and materials and then in the corner somewhere,
you're going to have a computer to do billing or something.
Well, there's a business office, the term business office,
broader than the term corporate office,
which denotes a corporate suite, you know,
where you have the various officers of the corporation.
Whereas with a small business, there is no suite,
but there's still office functions.
This has this definition of corporate office.
And the thing is a corporate office,
a corporation is a form of business, right?
It's a form of ownership of property
as compared to like a limited partnership or something.
There's no reference to the structure of the business in this definition.
So this would seem to be the best definition for constructing
what do they mean by professional or business office.
It's a place where you're conducting the affairs.
Now, place of location,
just where the place of business is not synonymous with an office.
Although, yes, if you have a place of business,
I do want to kind of tie in with one thing.
There is a statement made that in these businesses,
there's going to be essentially a mobile workshop
that they're going out in the field somewhere.
And that, you know, to the extent that they're doing warehousing
of materials and equipment in these bays,
that that's accessory.
But the issue is this is land use regulations.
We're looking at regulating a piece of land.
Nobody cares what someone's doing in Keen.
What's happening on the land is they're essentially,
primarily warehousing equipment and materials and tools
that they're going to then take out somewhere else
and do this workshop.
Well, wouldn't you agree with me that if this is approved
as use as a business office,
and one of the tenants is using the property in a way
that's inconsistent with that,
that the town would have enforcement ability
to remedy that situation.
You can't get approval for one use
and then engage in a different use that is more intensive
than what was originally represented to the ZBA, right?
